nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich lagoons and outer reef slopes between approximately 3 and 60 m, often in areas of high reef relief; juveniles tend to favour more sheltered inner reefs.

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding on sponges, tunicates and macroalgae. In aquaria a varied diet is generally advised, including Spirulina, marine algae sheets, high-quality angelfish preparations with sponge content, mysis and finely chopped frozen seafood. Multiple small feedings per day tend to be better tolerated than one large meal.

imageCandidates.notes: Leonard Low; 2400x1824. Tack-sharp full-body side profile of a vibrant adult showing all diagnostics: six dark vertical bars over a tan/yellow body dense with blue polka-dots, blue-spotted fins and dark head with white post-orbital bar. Cleanest, most complete framing.
Rickard Zerpe; 4336x3252. Sharp, high-res in-situ adult with intense blue spotting and blue-spotted tail, red gorgonian backdrop; different individual/angle for variety.
Rickard Zerpe; 3491x2618. Crisp, vibrant adult, excellent detail on blue-spot pattern and dark bars; small second fish in top corner. Strong alternate angle.

summary: The Six Bar Angelfish (Pomacanthus sexstriatus) is a large Indo-Pacific species reaching 40–46 cm in adulthood. It is generally considered semi-aggressive and is not reliably reef-safe, making it best suited to experienced keepers with very large aquariums.
careNotes: The Six Bar Angelfish (Pomacanthus sexstriatus) is one of the larger Pomacanthus angelfishes, reaching around 40-46 cm as an adult. It is generally advised to provide a minimum tank of around 220 US gallons (~833 L) with extensive live rock, deep caves for refuge and substantial open swimming space across a long footprint. Juveniles and adults display strikingly different colour patterns: juveniles are dark blue-black with curving white vertical bands, while adults show the characteristic dark vertical bars on a pale body with blue polka-dot fins, and the colour transition can take many months in captivity. It is generally considered reef-safe with caution as it has been reported to nip stony and soft corals as well as clam mantles. Keep only one per system and avoid combining with other large angelfishes of similar size. Like other large Pomacanthus species, it is reportedly prone to Head and Lateral Line Erosion when kept in poor water quality or on a vitamin-poor diet, so stable parameters and regular feeding of marine-algae-based foods are commonly considered important. Adults are known to produce loud grunting sounds when disturbed. The 15–20 year lifespan figure is drawn from typical longevity reports for large Pomacanthus species and should be treated as approximate.
breedingNotes: Not known to be bred in home aquaria. Like other large Pomacanthus, the species is widely reported to be a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (46 cm TL vs LiveAquaria 18"/45.7 cm — agreement), wild temperature range (25.5-29 degC mean 28.1 vs LiveAquaria captive 22-26 degC — middle range used), native distribution, family/order, and habitat depth range.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Sixbar angelfish)
additional.notes: Tertiary cross-check: confirmed max size ~46 cm, distribution from Sri Lanka to Solomon Islands and Ryukyus to Australia, family Pomacanthidae / order Acanthuriformes, omnivorous diet on macroalgae/sponges/tunicates, distinct juvenile colour phase, and grunting-when-disturbed behaviour.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite in the wild; captive breeding is essentially undocumented.
